## Plotting FRED Data and web scraping

### Eamon Espey

#### 9/19/24

To start, let's download consumer price index data from the Federal Reserve. Download all months available and make a plot with axis labels and a title

```{r}
# read in the data
path = 'U:/Documents/econ8600/9-17-24/'
cpi = read.csv(paste0(path, "MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE.csv"))

# change the date column to a date object
cpi$DATE = as.Date(cpi$DATE)

# make a plot of the data with axis labels and a title
plot_cpi = function() {plot(cpi$DATE, cpi$MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE, type='l', col='darkgray', xlab='', ylab='Percent Change at Annual Rate', main='Median Consumer Price Index')}
plot_cpi()
```

Add a shaded region for recessions (July 1990 - March 1991, March 2001 - November 2001, December 2007 - June 2009, February 2020 - April 2020)

```{r}
plot_cpi()
#par("usr")
# par("usr") gives us a vector of extreme values of plotting region in the order c(x1, x2, y1, y2) 
shade_rec = function(x1, x2) {
  rect(as.Date(x1), par("usr")[3], as.Date(x2), par("usr")[4], col = "#f7f7f7", border=NA)
  par(new=T)
}

plot_cpi_w_recessions = function() {
  plot_cpi()
  shade_rec("1990-7-1", "1991-3-1")
  shade_rec("2001-3-1", "2001-11-1")
  shade_rec("2007-12-1", "2009-6-1")
  shade_rec("2020-2-1", "2020-4-1")
  plot_cpi()
}
plot_cpi_w_recessions()
```

Add

```{r}

# make a plot of the data
plot_cpi_w_recessions()

# plot months with less than 2% inflation in green
under2 = cpi
under2$MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE[under2$MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE>2] = 2
lines(under2$DATE, under2$MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE, col='green3')
abline(2, 0, col='green3')

# plot months with greater than 4% inflation in green
over4 = cpi
over4$MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE[over4$MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE<4] = 4
lines(over4$DATE, over4$MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE, col='red2')
abline(4, 0, col='red2')

# add a line for the overall mean inflation rate
abline(mean(cpi$MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE), 0)

# add a legend
legend('topleft', legend=c("greater than 4% inflation", "long-term average inflation", "less than 2% inflation"), lty=1, col=c("red2", "black", "green3"), cex=0.8)
```

## Using the FRED API in R

First, create an account: https://fredaccount.stlouisfed.org/login/secure/

Go to your API Keys page

Request an API Key - for the description, I put "R scripts for accessing datasets"

Copy your API Key to a .txt file on your computer

Load the fredr library and set up your API Key

```{r}
library(fredr)

# path to your API Key
API_Key_path = "C:/Users/sgtur/OneDrive/Documents/FRED API KEY.txt"
# read the text file in which your API Key is stored
key = readLines(API_Key_path)

# set your API Key with fredr
fredr_set_key(key)
```

Recreate the cpi plots from before using the API:

```{r}
cpi = fredr(series_id = "MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE")

# make the column name match from the csv so the plot functions will still work...
names(cpi)[1] = "DATE"
names(cpi)[3] = "MEDCPIM158SFRBCLE"

# change the date column to a date object
cpi$DATE = as.Date(cpi$DATE)

# generate the plot
plot_cpi_w_recessions()
```

Recreate this plot:

![](images/clipboard-1197842575.png)

```{r}
# get GDP data from FRED
gdp_data <- na.omit(fredr(series_id = "GDP"))


# look at the first few rows
head(gdp_data)

# format date column
gdp_data$date = as.Date(gdp_data$date)

# make function to plot gdp
plot_gdp = function(){
  plot(gdp_data$date, gdp_data$value, type='l', ylab="Billions of Dollars", xlab="", main="US Gross Domestic Product", col='darkblue', lwd=2)
}

plot_gdp_w_recessions = function() {
  plot_gdp()
  shade_rec("1929-8-01", "1933-3-01")
  shade_rec("1937-5-01", "1938-6-01")
  shade_rec("1945-2-01", "1945-10-01")
  shade_rec("1948-11-01", "1949-10-01")
  shade_rec("1953-7-01", "1954-5-01")
  shade_rec("1957-8-01", "1958-4-01")
  shade_rec("1960-4-01", "1961-2-01")
  shade_rec("1969-12-01", "1970-11-01")
  shade_rec("1973-11-01", "1975-3-01")
  shade_rec("1980-1-01", "1980-7-01")
  shade_rec("1981-7-01", "1982-11-01")
  shade_rec("1990-7-1", "1991-3-1")
  shade_rec("2001-3-1", "2001-11-1")
  shade_rec("2007-12-1", "2009-6-1")
  shade_rec("2020-2-1", "2020-4-1")
  plot_gdp()
}
plot_gdp_w_recessions()
```


TO GET TABLES OFF OF WEBSITES
- if webscraping more than robots.txt ask. zB Zillow is really strict 
    - robots 
```{r}
# Load necessary libraries
library(rvest)

# URL of the Wikipedia page
url <- "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clemson_Tigers_football"

# Read the HTML of the page
page <- read_html(url)

# Extract all tables from the page
tables <- html_table(page, fill = TRUE)

# View a table
head(tables[[4]])
dim(tables[[4]]) #Dimensions

conf_champs = tables[[4]]

# how many conference championships did Danny Ford win?
print(paste("Danny Ford won", nrow(conf_champs[conf_champs$Coach=="Danny Ford",]), "conference championships."))

# In which years did Danny Ford win the conference championship?
conf_champs$Year[conf_champs$Coach=="Danny Ford"]
```

What is the average rating for the item linked below? How many reviews are there?

```{r}

# URL of the Wikipedia page
url <- "https://www.amazon.com/Web-Scraping-Python-Collecting-Modern/dp/1491985577"
urltutorial <- "https://cran.r-project.org/web//packages/stringr/vignettes/regular-expressions.html"

# Read the HTML of the page
page <- read_html(url)
pagetutorial <- read_html(urltutorial)
# Extract all text from the page INSTEAD of tables, text2
text = html_text2(page) 

# Write the text to a .txt file for easier inspection
write.table(text, "review.txt")

# load the stringr library for string extraction
library(stringr)

# extract the average rating  searching for specific object within the page. Regular expressions in r
rating = str_extract(text, pattern="[12345][\\.]?[:digit:]? out of 5 stars") #the \\ tell us to get

#Characters are anything including those in square bracket regardless of order or if one or the other str_extract(x,C[LX]) gives #> [1] "CLXXX"

#If \ is used as an escape character in regular expressions, how do you match a literal \? Well you need to escape it, creating the regular expression \\. To create that regular expression, you need to use a string, which also needs to escape \. That means to match a literal \ you need to write "\\\\" â€” you need four backslashes to match one!
#parse_number - finds the first number in a string

print(rating) 

# extract the number of reviews
num_reviews = str_extract(text, pattern="[:digit:]* ratings") #any number of digits that follows the _ ratings, *will only return first instance
#num_reviews = str_extract(text, pattern = "out of 5 stars [:digit:]*) Asterisk gives you any number after that sentence any number of d
print(num_reviews)

library(readr)
parse_number(rating)
parse_number(num_reviews)
```
\\\\ tells R: I want to search for 2 actual backslashes
Find the ages of the past five presidents:

```{r}
get_age = function(url) {
  page <- read_html(url)
  text = html_text2(page)
  age = parse_number(str_extract(text, pattern="\\(age [:digit:]{2,}*\\)")) #find patterns want to use on the website to get formatting.
} #squiggly bracket for at least 2.

path = "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/"
presidents = c("Joe_Biden", "Donald_Trump", "Barack_Obama", "George_W._Bush", "Bill_Clinton")

# method 1...
ages = numeric(length(presidents))

for (i in 1:length(presidents)) {
  ages[i] = get_age(paste0(path, presidents[i]))
}

pres_ages1 = data.frame(President = presidents, Age = ages)
print(pres_ages1)

#paste0 smooshes 2 strings together without spaces
# method 2... (faster)
pres_ages2 = data.frame(President = presidents, Age = unlist(lapply(paste0(path, presidents), get_age)))
print(pres_ages2)
```
